bull_thesis:
Insperity is a founder-led professional employer organization (PEO — a firm that handles payroll, benefits, and HR administration on behalf of small and mid-sized businesses) with a net-cash balance sheet of +$125 million as of March 31, 2026, and a return on invested capital (ROIC — net operating profit divided by the capital deployed to generate it) of 16.5%. The company's 2025 earnings collapse — net income fell from $91 million in 2024 to a loss of $7 million, and earnings per share (EPS) dropped from $2.39 to −$0.18 — was caused by an industry-wide spike in healthcare claims, not by a failure to retain clients. Benefits costs rose 9% year over year; insurer loss ratios at UnitedHealth and peers confirm the shock was sector-wide, not specific to Insperity. Critically, client retention held at 99% per month throughout 2025, with full-year retention of roughly 83%, up from 81% the prior year.

The margin recovery is contracted and mechanical, not speculative. Three levers are already in motion: (1) a new multi-year UnitedHealthcare contract, live January 1, 2026, cuts large-claim pooling to $500,000 and takes roughly 2% off the cost side; (2) benefit plan-design changes also took effect in January 2026; and (3) approximately 60% of the client book has yet to be repriced at higher, margin-recovery rates as contracts renew through 2026. Management has put numbers on the rebound: full-year 2026 adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) of $170–$230 million, up 30–76%, and adjusted EPS of $1.60–$2.60, up 55–152%.

The first post-shock data point validates the inflection. Q1 2026, reported in May 2026, printed adjusted EPS of $1.31 and adjusted EBITDA of $103 million; the year-over-year gross-profit decline narrowed sharply from −21% in Q4 2025 to just −3% in Q1 2026.

Beyond the self-help recovery, there is an unpriced 2027 option: the Insperity HRScale / Workday joint platform targeting mid-market companies with 150–5,000 employees, a total addressable market of roughly 40,000 businesses covering 25 million employees. Real revenue from HRScale is expected beginning 2027, as the approximately $58 million per year of investment spending rolls off. Insider conviction backs the thesis: 23 open-market purchases totaling 572,566 shares against only 7 sales.

At roughly $36.60, the stock trades at approximately 16–17 times the 2026 adjusted EPS midpoint of about $2.10, 6 times EV/EBITDA (enterprise value — market cap plus net debt — divided by EBITDA), and 1.3 times EV/GP (enterprise value divided by gross profit). PEO peers — TriNet, ADP TotalSource, and Paychex — trade at 18–25 times earnings. The market is pricing Insperity as a value trap while its customer base is intact, its recovery is contractually underway, and its first 2026 quarter has already confirmed the turn.
bear_thesis:
Insperity is a no-growth PEO whose core unit engine is shrinking. Management guided worksite employee (WSEE — the number of employees Insperity administers on behalf of client companies) growth of −1.5% to +1.5% for 2026; third-party forecasts point to a 1–2.3% decline in absolute headcount to 303,000–307,000. Part of the "margin recovery" is being manufactured by deliberately terminating unprofitable client relationships — what management called "a higher number of company-initiated nonrenewals" on the Q4 2025 earnings call.

The 2025 collapse was real. EPS round-tripped from $4.64 in 2022 to −$0.18 in 2025, and management missed its own benefits-cost forecasts in four consecutive quarters, with a beat rate of 3 out of 7 quarters (43%) — below a coin flip. The 2026 guidance range ($1.60–$2.60 adjusted EPS) is unusually wide precisely because the healthcare cost line remains difficult to forecast. If inflation in medical claims re-accelerates in 2026 — and the AI-driven upcoding of medical bills that management flagged in Q3 2025 is a structural, industry-wide trend that is getting worse — EPS lands at the low end of the range and the "recovery" is a mirage.

The HRScale platform, described as the growth engine, is entirely unquantified, deferred to 2027, and dependent on a third-party relationship with Workday — which itself underwent a leadership change in Q4 2025. It is a promise, not a number.

Capital returns — roughly $90 million in dividends and $19 million in buybacks in 2025 — were funded through a loss year, pulling adjusted cash down to $57 million before management expanded the credit facility from $650 million to $750 million and raised the maximum leverage ceiling from 3.0 to 3.75 times EBITDA. Trailing twelve-month (TTM — the most recent four reported quarters added together) free cash flow is only $67 million, and TTM diluted EPS is −$0.67.

Strip away the recovery hope and you have a flat, ex-growth, aging-founder PEO that, on current financials, deserves a low-teens earnings multiple. JPMorgan rates the stock Underweight with a $33 price target, below the current price. The thesis fails if Q2 or Q3 2026 shows benefits-cost re-acceleration, a client attrition surge from repricing, or HRScale slipping past 2027.
consensus_delta: The key disagreement with the market: the street is pricing permanent margin impairment into a business whose earnings power is recovering. JPMorgan carries an Underweight rating with a $33 price target below the current price, and the consensus is Hold. That view extrapolates the 2025 healthcare shock as the new run-rate. The case against that view rests on three points. First, the shock was industry-wide and exogenous — insurer loss-ratio blowups at UnitedHealth and peers confirm this was not an Insperity underwriting failure. Second, the customer base never left: 99% monthly retention, full-year retention of roughly 83%. Third, the recovery is contracted and has already shown up in the numbers — Q1 2026 gross-profit decline narrowed from −21% to −3%. The street is anchored on the trough income statement and the negative WSEE guidance while underweighting a 55–152% earnings rebound that the first quarter of the year already corroborated. The mispricing is the gap between the current roughly 16 times trough-recovery multiple and the 18–25 times range where PEO peers trade on normalized earnings.
forcing_function: There is no single hard date that shatters consensus — the re-rating is driven by serial quarterly proof, which weakens the urgency versus a clean catalyst that forces an immediate re-pricing. The key dates: Q2 2026 earnings, expected around early August 2026, will be the second consecutive quarter of benefits-cost stabilization and margin recovery — the key incremental proof point. Q3 2026, around early November 2026, is when a larger share of the roughly 60% of the book still awaiting repricing will have renewed, which should narrow the full-year adjusted EPS range upward. The full-year 2026 result and 2027 guidance, expected around February 2027, is the moment HRScale revenue and the Workday spending roll-off enter the model, converting the 2027 option into a real number. The catalyst that forces the re-rating is therefore a cadence of quarterly confirmations, not a binary event — which is precisely why this is a watchlist position rather than an aggressive entry.
valley_of_death: The risky window covers roughly the next 3–9 months before the re-rating completes. Four specific risks warrant attention. First, benefits-cost re-acceleration: this is the single line management could not forecast for four straight quarters; the AI-driven upcoding of medical bills is a structural, worsening trend that could push 2026 EPS to the low end of the $1.60–$2.60 range and stall the re-rating. Watch Q2 (August 2026) and Q3 (November 2026) benefits trends closely. Second, a client attrition spike: repricing roughly 60% of the book while simultaneously firing unprofitable clients is a real retention risk; the headline WSEE count is already guided negative for 2026, which can spook the market each quarter even if per-client economics improve. Third, dividend strain: the roughly $90 million annual dividend run-rate was funded through a loss year; adjusted cash dropped to $57 million before the credit facility was upsized, and TTM free cash flow is only $67 million — a weak first half in 2026 could pressure the dividend narrative even though net cash is still positive at +$125 million. Fourth, and somewhat reassuring: there is no debt maturity wall (the facility runs to December 2028) and no forced-selling dynamic, so the risk here is earnings volatility, not a solvency event.
positioning_washout: There is limited forced-selling pressure here. Insperity is not primarily a yield-driven or passive-index holding, and no dividend cut appears imminent, so there is no likely wave of income-oriented shareholders being flushed out. The more important positioning observation runs in the opposite direction: the stock is under-owned by investors who would benefit from the recovery. JPMorgan is Underweight, consensus is Hold, and institutional "smart money" ownership scores only 32 out of 100. The forced-selling pressure that matters is the gradual capitulation of the value-trap thesis as each 2026 quarter delivers the recovery — a slow process, not a single liquidation event. Insider open-market buying (23 purchases totaling 572,566 shares against only 7 sales) signals that the people closest to the business are accumulating, the opposite of a forced-seller dynamic.
moderator_conclusion:
Insperity is a genuine deep-value PEO recovery. It is a founder-led, net-cash (+$125 million), 16.5%-ROIC annuity business that absorbed an industry-wide healthcare-claims shock in 2025, kept 99% of its customers per month, and is executing a contracted, mechanical margin recovery — a new UHC contract live since January 2026, roughly 60% of the book still to be repriced — that the first post-shock quarter (Q1 2026: gross-profit decline narrowed from −21% to −3%, adjusted EPS $1.31) has already validated. The forensic picture is clean: management credibility 4 of 5; real insider buying; no solvency wall; peer-discount valuation at 16–17 times 2026 mid-EPS against an 18–25 times PEO peer range. On pure value merits, this is a strong setup: base-case sum-of-the-parts of roughly $41 versus a stock price of roughly $36.60, bull case $46–$48, conservative bear $30–$32 — favorable asymmetry with a partly de-risked floor.

That said, applying the current investment regime, which rewards hard-dated, contractually binding catalysts and structural special situations in distressed, spinoff, or forced-seller situations, and penalizes recovery stories whose catalyst is a slow multi-quarter grind: NSP's catalyst is exactly that. It is a dated but soft catalyst, no contractual payout — the binary question of whether the business recovers has been answered yes, and what remains is a re-rating that depends on serial quarterly proof. The HRScale leg is third-party dependent, unquantified, and deferred to 2027. There is no proxy fight, no asset sale, no forced-seller mechanic — none of the regime's most compelling entry setups. That caps the regime-adjusted conviction at 3 and the verdict at 'wait for the wash-out': the thesis is real and the value is there, but the cleanest entry is on a benefits-cost scare or WSEE-driven flush — watch Q2 2026, around August — that lets the recovery crowd buy in below roughly $33 before the re-rating compounds. Down-rated from a value-only conviction of 4 to a regime-adjusted conviction of 3 because the catalyst is partly spent and lacks a hard forcing event; value conviction stays at 4 because the sum-of-the-parts-versus-price gap and forensic quality stand on their own regardless of catalyst timing.
sop_breakdown: Intrinsic value estimated using an EV/GP (enterprise value divided by gross profit) methodology, cross-checked against a normalized earnings multiple, with a net-cash overlay. For the PEO core: normalized through-cycle gross profit of approximately $1.0 billion, multiplied by a 1.4 times EV/GP multiple — a mid-cycle figure between the bear-case 1.2 times distressed multiple and the 1.5 times peer-normal multiple, discounted modestly for the shrinking WSEE base — produces an enterprise value of roughly $1.40 billion. Adding $125 million of net cash (verified from the March 31, 2026 balance sheet, where cash of $555 million exceeds total debt of $430 million) gives equity value of roughly $1.53 billion, or approximately $41 per share on roughly 37 million shares outstanding. The earnings cross-check — 2026 midpoint adjusted EPS of about $2.10 times roughly 17 times, plus roughly $3 of net cash per share — produces $39–$41, consistent with the gross-profit method. HRScale is carried at zero in the base case; it is a 2027 option, not yet a number in the model. The roughly $90 million annual dividend run-rate provides yield support but was funded through a loss year and is therefore not added as a separate valuation component.
risk_reward: Favorable asymmetry, but the path is a grind rather than a sharp binary re-rating event. On the downside, from roughly $36.60 to the conservative bear-case sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) of $30–$32 is a loss of roughly 13–18%; a stall scenario where the multiple compresses to a low-teens level implies a roughly $24 floor, a 34% decline. On the upside, the base-case fair value of roughly $41 represents a gain of roughly 12%; the bull case of $46–$48 represents a gain of 26–31%. The central-scenario asymmetry is approximately 1.5–2.0 times to the upside. The key edge is that the recovery catalyst has already begun firing — Q1 2026 confirmed the inflection — yet the stock has not re-rated, which means the downside floor is partly de-risked while the re-rating is still ahead.
catalyst_status: SOFT_EXTENDED (recovery half-FIRED, re-rate still pending). The load-bearing catalyst is the 2026 margin recovery (UHC contract + repricing of ~60% of the book + plan-design changes). Web-verified as of 2026-06: the UnitedHealthcare multi-year contract is LIVE (effective Jan-1-2026, large-claim pooling cut to $500K, ~2% cost reduction); Q1 2026 results (reported ~May-2026) already CONFIRMED the inflection — adj EPS $1.31, adj EBITDA $103M, gross-profit YoY decline narrowing from -21% to -3% — so the proof-of-recovery event has happened. BUT the multiple has NOT re-rated: stock still ~$33-37, JPMorgan Underweight $33 PT, consensus Hold (avg PT ~$47.67). So the catalyst is a multi-quarter grind that is partly spent (the binary 'does it recover' is resolved YES) but whose re-rating is incomplete and depends on serial quarterly proof. peer_comps_note: Peer comparison verdict: in line with the sector. Insperity competes against TriNet (ticker TNET), ADP TotalSource, Paychex TotalSource, and global staffing firms Randstad and Manpower. The 13.2% gross margin is characteristic of PEOs, where gross profit is calculated net of pass-through payroll taxes; the 16.5% ROIC is solid and roughly comparable to TriNet's approximately 18%. The near-zero free-cash-flow multiple and near-breakeven net margin flagged in the peer comparison reflect the 2025 healthcare-shock trough, not a structural problem; trailing twelve-month free cash flow has already recovered to $67 million and the 2026 guidance implies a return to roughly $2 per share of earnings power. PEO peers trade at 18–25 times earnings (TriNet at roughly 18 times); at roughly 16–17 times the 2026 adjusted EPS midpoint, Insperity sits at a discount to the peer band — that discount is the source of the re-rating opportunity. The 1.4 times EV/GP base multiple is conservative relative to where a recovered-margin PEO with this ROIC profile and net-cash balance sheet would normally trade.
interrogator_dossier:
Insperity, Inc. (NSP) — Forensic Dossier

Source transcripts: Q1 2025 (April 29, 2025), Q2 2025 (August 1, 2025), Q3 2025 (November 4, 2025), Q4 and full-year 2025 (February 10, 2026). Financials: screener scan plus third-party financial data through March 31, 2026. Investment signal type: deep value. Valuation methodology: EV/GP (enterprise value divided by gross profit).


Narrative Arc

The story across these four quarters is the anatomy of a margin shock and management's response to it — not a growth story but a damage-control-and-recovery story.

Q1 2025 (the shock lands): The call opened with what management framed as an inflection point — "we achieved an important inflection point, reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees" — but was immediately overshadowed by two blows. First, a tariff- and policy-driven collapse in small-business sentiment ("66% of respondents expect the economic climate to have a negative effect on their business this year, up from only 29% in January"). Second, a benefits-cost explosion: "benefits costs exceeded our budget by $28 million," with $12 million of that described as an "unprecedented" prior-period claims-runoff adjustment. The full-year benefits-cost growth guidance was raised from 5.0–6.5% to 6.5–7.5%. The forward narrative pivoted to pricing realignment by January 2026 and the Workday strategic partnership as the growth engine.

Q2 2025 (the shock is not transitory): Benefits costs missed again by roughly $12 million, now driven by pharmacy and GLP-1 drug costs. Management explicitly debated whether this was "a longer-term new normal" and landed on conservatism. The Workday offering was named — Insperity HRScale — and the flagship PEO product was rebranded HR 360. The dominant language shifted from "growth" to "profitability improvement in 2026" via pricing increases, plan-design changes, and the anticipated UHC contract renewal. The three-lever recovery plan crystallized here.

Q3 2025 (capitulation on the cost trend): Benefits costs overshot forecasts by $20 million; adjusted EPS went negative at −$0.20. Management conceded that "the prevailing view in the industry is that the higher claims trend experienced in 2025 will persist in 2026" — and introduced a new causal factor: the use of AI tools by healthcare providers driving up medical coding and billing, known as upcoding. HRScale officially rolled out. The 2026 outlook hardened into "financial performance rebound and growth acceleration."

Q4 and full-year 2025 (the pivot to recovery): Adjusted EPS came in at −$0.60; the year closed with a net loss. But the narrative was entirely forward-looking: a new multi-year UHC contract (large-claim pooling cut to $500,000, roughly 2% cost reduction, effective January 1, 2026), benefit plan-design changes, and pricing — "approximately 60% of our current client base are yet to receive applicable pricing upon their renewal dates over the course of the year." Management right-sized the organization (a 4% non-sales headcount reduction, accelerated office consolidation), expanded the credit facility from $650 million to $750 million with the maximum leverage ceiling moving from 3.0 to 3.75 times, and explicitly lowered the 2026 unit-growth bar to −1.5% to +1.5% WSEE. The thesis had fully migrated from "grow" to "recover margin in 2026, re-accelerate growth in 2027 on HRScale."

Net narrative arc: A clean, sequential deterioration in 2025 results paired with an increasingly specific, increasingly credible recovery plan for 2026. Growth drivers were quietly deprioritized; the load-bearing driver became self-help margin recovery, with HRScale as a 2027 option.


Claims Versus Financials

Management claim: "reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees" (Q1 2025). The numbers: WSEE growth was +0.7% in Q1, +0.7% in Q2, +1.2% in Q3, +1.1% in Q4 — barely positive, then guided to −1.5% to +1.5% for 2026. Assessment: technically true but trivially so; growth is effectively flat to declining. The narrative was richer than the numbers.

Management claim: benefits costs are a manageable, partly transitory issue (Q1–Q2 2025). The numbers: full-year net income collapsed from $91 million in 2024 to a loss of $7 million in 2025; net margin fell to −0.1%; EPS moved from $2.39 to −$0.18. Assessment: the numbers were far worse than the early framing. The Q3 capitulation — conceding that the trend would persist in 2026 — finally matched reality.

Management claim: "we have raised our pricing targets ... realign our pricing by January" (Q1 2025 forward). The numbers: revenue per WSEE per month rose roughly 3% in Q1 2026 per subsequent data; but gross profit per WSEE fell from $338 to $208 by Q3 before stabilizing at $183 in Q4. Assessment: pricing actions were real but lagged the cost curve all year. The price-cost gap only began closing at year-end.

Management claim: strong client retention is "a bright spot" (all quarters). The numbers: retention held at 99% per month all year; full-year retention of roughly 83% versus roughly 81% the prior year. Assessment: corroborated. The annuity book held — the single most important positive signal in the financials.

Management claim: capital returns intact (all quarters). The numbers: $90 million in dividends and $19 million in buybacks paid in 2025 into a net-loss year; adjusted cash ended Q4 at only $57 million before the credit facility was expanded. Assessment: true, but funded through a loss year — aggressive capital allocation. The net-cash position of +$125 million as of March 31, 2026 shows the balance sheet absorbed the strain.

Management claim: 2026 will bring "rebound and improved profitability" (Q2–Q4 2025 forward). The numbers: full-year 2026 guidance (issued Q4) of adjusted EBITDA $170–$230 million (up 30–76%) and adjusted EPS $1.60–$2.60 (up 55–152%); ROIC already at 16.5%; net cash +$125 million. Q1 2026 actuals — adjusted EPS $1.31, adjusted EBITDA $103 million, gross-profit decline narrowing from −21% to −3% — corroborate the guide. Assessment: the recovery claim is now quantified, dated, and supported by the first post-shock quarter. Numbers are finally catching up to the narrative.

Where the story diverged from the numbers: throughout all of 2025, as management's "rebound is coming" ran ahead of a still-deteriorating income statement for four straight quarters. Where the two now align: the 2026 guidance is concrete, and the first post-reset data point (Q1 2026) confirms the inflection.


Tone and Confidence Over Time

Trajectory: declining through 2025, inflecting upward at Q4, net stable-to-rising on the forward outlook.

Q1 2025 — surprised and defensive: "We are obviously disappointed by the emergence of higher benefits costs"; described as an "unprecedented occurrence." Confidence wobbled but stayed forward-looking.

Q2 2025 — managing expectations down: "These results fell slightly under the low end of our forecasted range ... primarily due to a continuation of higher-than-expected benefits costs." Tone was methodical rather than panicked: "the challenges we are facing are not unique to Insperity."

Q3 2025 — most defensive: negative adjusted EPS; the industry-attribution framing peaked (AI-upcoding); explicit capitulation to "trend persists in 2026." But a hard pivot to "financial performance rebound" also appeared here.

Q4 2025 — re-asserting control: "we believe [these actions] position us for a significant recovery in profitability this year"; decisive actions on the UHC contract, headcount, and the credit facility. Language flipped back to offense on margin while remaining defensive on unit growth ("lowers our view of projected growth for 2026 by around 3%").

Guidance specificity increased over time. By Q4, management provided a full-year EBITDA and EPS range plus a "60% of book yet to reprice" roadmap — far more granular than the hand-wavy "realign by January" framing of Q1. Classic pattern: offensive on growth and investment spending early in 2025, pivoting to defensive cost management and margin recovery by Q4, with a credible offensive-on-margin re-assertion at year-end.


Guidance Reliability

Rating: medium reliability, improving. The 2025 track record is a string of misses. Q1 2025 results fell below guidance (benefits). Q2 2025 fell slightly under the low end of the range by $0.03 and $1 million. Q3 2025 fell below forecasted ranges. Q4 2025 adjusted EPS of −$0.54 ex-charge was "near the middle" of forecasted ranges — the first in-line print, notably after the bar had been reset lower. The screener beat rate across seven quarters is 3 out of 7 (43%), consistent with the miss streak.

This is not a sandbagging company; 2025 was genuine serial disappointment as the healthcare cost curve kept outrunning management's estimates. The credibility upgrade is mechanical: by Q4 they reset to a low, wide base, and the Q1 2026 actual landed cleanly inside the new framework — the first sign that the guidance is now set conservatively. Management's stated philosophy — "be conservative because we want to be sure" — appears to finally be holding at the reset level. Rating: medium, trending up only because the first post-reset quarter delivered.


Analyst Pressure Points

"Is this benefits trend the new normal?" This question was pressed every quarter by JPMorgan's analyst and others. Management deflected toward "transitory and mix-driven" in Q1 and Q2, then conceded persistence in Q3. The analysts who pushed early were right; management was slow to acknowledge reality.

UHC contract economics: repeatedly probed, particularly on pricing risk-sharing mechanics. Management gave only qualitative "alignment" answers through Q2, then revealed hard terms at Q4 — $500,000 pooling threshold, roughly 2% cost reduction. The deflection resolved favorably: a rare case where the non-answer turned into good news.

Workday and HRScale profitability: analysts pushed on margin contribution and the roughly $150 million aggregate investment. Management consistently declined to quantify revenue or EBITDA contribution ("we haven't locked that down yet" in Q1; "we don't have a better number right now than $10 million a year going forward" in Q3). This remains the key open non-answer — HRScale economics are unquantified and deferred to 2027.

WSEE and net-hiring weakness: analysts repeatedly probed the sluggish client hiring data. Management characterized it as macro-driven and small-business-sentiment-driven, not company-specific, and notably stated they had not yet seen AI-driven job replacement at their clients (Q3) — flagging it as a forward risk they cannot yet size.


Strengths and Concerns

Concerns: Net income round-tripped from $179 million in 2022 to a loss of $7 million in 2025, with EPS falling from $4.64 to −$0.18 — a real earnings-power impairment, not a cosmetic one. Four consecutive guidance misses in 2025, driven by a healthcare cost line management could not forecast for nine months. Dividends and buybacks funded through a loss year; adjusted cash fell to $57 million at Q4 before recovering; maximum leverage raised to 3.75 times — capital-return discipline was strained. HRScale economics entirely unquantified and deferred to 2027; third-party dependent on Workday, which itself had a leadership change. WSEE unit growth guided negative for 2026; part of the margin recovery is being achieved by deliberately shedding lower-margin clients.

Strengths: 99% monthly client retention held all year — the annuity moat is intact despite aggressive repricing. The new multi-year UHC contract carries hard, favorable terms (large-claim pooling cut to $500,000, roughly 2% cost reduction, live January 1, 2026), directly de-risking the gross-margin recovery. Roughly 60% of the book is still to be repriced through 2026 — a mechanical, contracted margin tailwind. Net-cash balance sheet of +$125 million as of March 31, 2026; ROIC of 16.5% — the capital-light PEO economics are intact. Real insider conviction: 23 open-market purchases totaling 572,566 shares against only 7 sales. Cost discipline was executed: operating expenses were down roughly 3% in 2025; headcount and office footprint were rationalized; Workday spending is declining.


Subtle Signals Worth Noting

The "AI-upcoding" attribution in Q3 2025 is a tell. Management reaching for an exogenous, industry-wide explanation — "we have recently been made aware" — rather than owning the underwriting miss. It functions to make the trend feel external and sector-wide (hence "everyone will reprice"), which is partly true given insurer loss-ratio blowups elsewhere, but is also a narrative shield.

Vocabulary shifted over the year: "growth and inflection" in Q1 became "margin recovery and profitability rebound" by Q3–Q4. By Q4, "margin recovery is the centerpiece of 2026" — growth language was explicitly deferred to 2027.

Capital-allocation reframing: the CFO's Q2 observation that the Workday spending "should never be going through the income statement ... it's an investment for the long term" was a soft setup for analysts to add it back. By Q3, management began capitalizing a portion of the investment as the accounting threshold was met — a real change that flatters reported 2026 operating expenses.

Client-mix management, stated quietly: "a higher number of company-initiated nonrenewals" in Q4 means management is deliberately terminating unprofitable client relationships. This suppresses the WSEE count — the headline unit metric the market watches — while improving per-WSEE economics. A deliberate trade of optics for margin quality.

No leadership change: Paul Sarvadi (Chairman and CEO, 40-year founder) and Douglas Sharp (CFO) remained in place through all four quarters. Continuity of voice makes the consistency of the recovery message more credible.


Summary Assessment for Portfolio Sizing

Classification: trajectory turning. Insperity is moving from a stalled-growth PEO into a self-help margin-recovery story, with a credible contracted tailwind in 2026 and a 2027 HRScale option layered on top.

This is a founder-led, net-cash, 16.5%-ROIC annuity business that took a genuine 2025 earnings hit from an industry-wide healthcare-cost shock, did not lose its customers (99% monthly retention), and is now executing a contracted, mechanical margin recovery: a new UHC contract live since January 2026, plan-design changes, and roughly 60% of the book still to reprice. The 2026 guidance of adjusted EPS $1.60–$2.60 represents a 55–152% earnings rebound, and the first post-shock quarter (Q1 2026, adjusted EPS $1.31) already confirms the inflection.

The single most important question before sizing a position: the recovery is real and largely already in motion and visible — is it still mispriced? The stock trades at roughly $33–$37 against a 2026 adjusted EPS midpoint of roughly $2.10 (approximately 16–17 times), while normalized through-cycle earning power is well above that; peers TriNet, ADP TotalSource, and Paychex trade at 18–25 times. The bull case requires benefits-cost trends to stop worsening (Q1 2026 says they are) and the market to re-rate a recovering, capital-light annuity it currently prices as a value trap. The bear case is that 2026 EPS lands at the low end ($1.60) if healthcare inflation re-accelerates, WSEE keeps shrinking, and HRScale never materializes — leaving a flat, ex-growth PEO at a deserved low-teens multiple. The asymmetry favors the bull, but the catalyst is a grind, not a binary, and it has already started firing.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory turning; moat narrow but real; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.

interrogator_dossier:
# Insperity, Inc. (NSP) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Bundled transcripts: Q1 2025 (2025-04-29), Q2 2025 (2025-08-01), Q3 2025 (2025-11-04), Q4/FY2025 (2026-02-10). Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM through 2026-03-31. Signal type: deep_value. Methodology: EV/GP.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these four quarters is the anatomy of a **margin shock and the management response to it** — not a growth story, a damage-control-and-recovery story.

- **Q1 2025 (the shock lands):** The call opens with what management framed as an *inflection* — "we achieved an important inflection point, reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees" — but is immediately overrun by two blows: (1) a tariff/policy-driven collapse in SMB sentiment ("66% of respondents expect the economic climate to have a negative effect on their business this year… up from only 29% in January"), and (2) a benefits-cost explosion: "benefits costs exceeded our budget by $28 million," with $12M of it an "unprecedented" prior-period claims-runoff adjustment. The full-year benefits-cost guide is raised from 5.0–6.5% to 6.5–7.5%. The forward narrative pivots to *pricing realignment by January 2026* and the *Workday strategic partnership* as the growth engine.

- **Q2 2025 (the shock is not transitory):** Benefits costs miss again ("$12 million" over forecast, now pharmacy/GLP-1-led). Management explicitly debates whether this is "a longer-term new normal" and lands on conservatism. The Workday offering is now named — **Insperity HRScale** — flagship PEO rebranded **HR 360**. The dominant verb shifts from "growth" to "**profitability improvement in 2026**" via "pricing increases, plan design changes and the negotiation of the anticipated renewal of our contract with UHC." The three-lever recovery plan crystallizes here.

- **Q3 2025 (capitulation on the cost trend):** Benefits cost overshoots by $20M; adjusted EPS goes negative (−$0.20). Management concedes "the prevailing view in the industry is that the higher claims trend experienced in 2025 will persist in 2026" — and introduces a striking new causal factor: "**the use of AI tools by health care providers**" driving upcoding. HRScale officially rolls out. The 2026 framing hardens into "**financial performance rebound and growth acceleration**."

- **Q4/FY2025 (the pivot to recovery as the whole thesis):** Adjusted EPS −$0.60; the year closes with a net loss. But the narrative is now entirely forward: a **new multi-year UHC contract** (large-claim pooling cut to $500K, ~2% cost-side reduction effective Jan-1-2026), benefit-plan-design changes, and pricing — "**approximately 60% of our current client base are yet to receive applicable pricing upon their renewal dates over the course of the year.**" Management rightsizes (4% of non-sales staff cut, accelerated sales-office consolidation), expands the credit facility ($650M→$750M, max leverage 3x→3.75x), and explicitly lowers the 2026 growth bar (−1.5% to +1.5% WSEE). The thesis has fully migrated from "grow" to "**recover margin in 2026, re-accelerate growth in 2027 on HRScale.**"

**Net arc:** A clean, sequential degradation of 2025 results paired with an increasingly specific, increasingly credible recovery plan for 2026. Growth drivers (HR360 net-hiring) were quietly dropped to near-zero; the load-bearing driver became *self-help margin recovery*, with HRScale as a 2027 option.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | The hard numbers say | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees" | Q1'25 | WSEE growth +0.7% Q1, +0.7% Q2, +1.2% Q3, +1.1% Q4 — *barely* positive, then guided to **−1.5% to +1.5% for 2026** | Technically true but trivially so; growth is effectively flat-to-down. Story richer than numbers. |
| Benefits costs a manageable, partly-transitory issue | Q1→Q2'25 | FY net income collapsed **$91M (2024) → −$7M (2025)**; net margin −0.1%; EPS $2.39 → **−$0.18** | The numbers are far worse than the early "spike that goes away" framing. The Q3 capitulation ("will persist in 2026") finally matched reality. |
| "we have raised our pricing targets… realign our pricing by January" | Q1'25 fwd | Revenue/WSEE/month: rising (+3% in Q1'26 per later data); gross profit/WSEE **fell** $338→$208 (Q3) before stabilizing $183 (Q4) | Pricing actions are real but lagged the cost curve all year. The price/cost gap only began closing at year-end. |
| Strong client retention "a bright spot" | Q1–Q4'25 | Retention 99%/month through the year; **full-year retention ~83%** (vs ~81% prior yr) | Corroborated. The annuity book held — the single most important green flag in the financials. |
| Capital returns intact | all qtrs | $90M dividends + $19M buybacks paid in 2025 **into a net loss**; ended Q4 with only $57M adjusted cash, drew up facility | True, but funded through a loss year — aggressive. Net-cash position (+$125M as of 2026-03-31) shows the balance sheet absorbed it. |
| 2026 "rebound… improved profitability" | Q2→Q4'25 | Guidance (issued Q4): adj EBITDA **$170–230M** (+30–76%), adj EPS **$1.60–2.60** (+55–152%); ROIC already 16.5%, net cash +$125M | The recovery claim is now *quantified and dated*, and Q1'26 actuals (adj EPS $1.31, adj EBITDA $103M, gross-profit decline narrowing −21%→−3%) corroborate it. Numbers now catching up to the narrative. |

**Where the story diverged from numbers:** all of 2025 — management's "rebound is coming" outran a still-deteriorating P&L for four straight quarters. **Where it now matches:** the 2026 guide is concrete, and the *first post-bundle datapoint (Q1 2026) confirms the inflection*, which materially upgrades credibility versus what the bundled transcripts alone would justify.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Declining through 2025, inflecting up at Q4 → net STABLE-to-rising on forward posture.**

- **Q1'25 — surprised/defensive:** "We are obviously disappointed by the emergence of higher benefits costs"; an "unprecedented occurrence." Confidence wobbles but stays forward-looking.
- **Q2'25 — managing-down:** "These results fell slightly under the low end of our forecasted range… primarily due to a continuation of higher-than-expected benefits costs." Tone is methodical, not panicked: "the challenges we are facing are not unique to Insperity."
- **Q3'25 — most defensive:** negative EPS, the industry-blame framing peaks (AI-upcoding), capitulation to "trend persists in 2026." But pivots hard to "**financial performance rebound**."
- **Q4'25 — re-asserting control:** "we believe position us for a significant recovery in profitability this year"; decisive actions (UHC contract, headcount cut, facility expansion). The language flips back to **offensive on margin** while staying **defensive on growth** ("lowers our view of projected growth for 2026 by around 3%").

Guidance specificity *increased* over time — by Q4 they gave a full-year EBITDA/EPS range and a "60% of book yet to reprice" roadmap, more granular than the hand-wavy Q1 "realign by January." Classic shift from **offensive (growth/Workday-investment)** to **defensive (cost/pricing/efficiency)** through 2025, with a credible offensive-on-margin re-assertion at Q4.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM (improving).** The 2025 track record is a string of misses:

- Q1'25 results "**fell below our guidance range**" (benefits).
- Q2'25 "**fell slightly under the low end**… by $0.03 and $1M."
- Q3'25 "**fell below our forecasted ranges**."
- Q4'25 adj EPS −$0.54 ex-charge was "**near the middle of our forecasted ranges**" — the first in-line print, notably *after* they had reset the bar low.
- Screener beat-rate: **3/7 quarters (43%)** — sub-coin-flip, consistent with the miss streak.

This is **not** a sandbagging name; 2025 was genuine serial disappointment as the cost curve kept outrunning their estimate. The credibility upgrade is mechanical: by Q4 they reset to a *low, wide* base, and the Q1'26 actual (adj EPS $1.31) landed cleanly inside the new framework — the first sign the new guide is set conservatively. Management's self-described approach — "be conservative because… we want to be sure" (Q1'25) — finally appears to be holding the line at the reset level. Score **Medium**, trending up only because the first post-reset quarter delivered.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"Is this benefits trend the new normal?"** — pressed every quarter (Polkowitz/JPM, Marcon). Management deflected toward "transitory/mix" in Q1–Q2, then conceded persistence in Q3. The analysts who pushed early were right; management was slow.
- **UHC contract economics** — repeatedly probed (Polkowitz Q1 & Q2: "what has been the outcomes in the past… price risk sharing?"). Management gave only qualitative "alignment" answers until the Q4 reveal of hard terms ($500K pooling, ~2% cost cut). The deflection resolved *favorably* — a rare case where the non-answer became good news.
- **Workday/HRScale profitability and investment cadence** — Jeff Martin (ROTH) and Polkowitz pushed on margin contribution and the "$150M aggregate investment." Management consistently refused to quantify revenue/EBITDA contribution ("we haven't locked that down yet," Q1; "we don't have a better number right now than $10M a year going forward," Q3). **This is the still-open non-answer** — HRScale economics remain unquantified and pushed to 2027.
- **WSEE/net-hiring weakness** — analysts kept probing the anemic client hiring; management consistently characterized it as macro/SMB-sentiment-driven, not company-specific, and notably said they've "**haven't seen any**" AI-driven job replacement yet (Q3) — a forward risk they flagged but can't size.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Net income round-tripped to a loss** — $179M (2022) → −$7M (2025); EPS $4.64 → −$0.18. A real earnings-power impairment, not cosmetic.
🔴 **Four consecutive guidance misses in 2025** driven by a cost line management could not forecast for nine months.
🔴 **Dividends + buybacks funded through a loss year**; adjusted cash fell to $57M at Q4 before recovering; raised max leverage to 3.75x — capital-return discipline strained.
🔴 **HRScale economics entirely unquantified and deferred to 2027** — the "growth engine" is a promise, not yet a number; third-party (Workday) dependent, and Workday itself had a "leadership change" (noted Q4).
🔴 **WSEE growth guided negative for 2026** — the core unit engine is shrinking; margin recovery is being achieved partly by *shedding* lower-margin clients ("company-initiated nonrenewals").

🟢 **99%/month client retention held all year** — the annuity moat is intact despite aggressive repricing.
🟢 **New multi-year UHC contract with hard, favorable terms** (pooling to $500K, ~2% cost reduction, live Jan-1-2026) — directly de-risks the gross-margin recovery.
🟢 **~60% of the book still to be repriced through 2026** — a mechanical, contracted margin tailwind, not a hope.
🟢 **Genuine net-cash balance sheet** (+$125M as of 2026-03-31; ROIC 16.5%) — capital-light PEO economics intact.
🟢 **Real insider conviction** — 23 open-market buys (572,566 sh) vs 7 sells — the strongest non-financial signal in the file.
🟢 **Cost discipline executed** — opex down ~3% in 2025, headcount/office rationalization, Workday spend falling.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The "AI-upcoding" attribution (Q3'25)** is a tell — management reaching for an exogenous, industry-wide explanation ("we have recently been made aware…") rather than owning the underwrite. It functions to make the trend feel *not-their-fault* and *industry-wide* (hence "everyone will reprice"), which is partly true (UNH/insurer loss-ratio blowups corroborate it) but also a narrative shield.

- **Capital-allocation reframing:** Paul's Q2 musing that Workday spend "should never be going through the income statement… it's an investment for the long term of capital" — soft-pedaling the earnings drag and pre-conditioning analysts to add it back. This depresses the WSEE count (the headline metric the market watches) while *improving* the per-WSEE economics — a deliberate trade of optics for margin.
- **No CEO/CFO change** — Sarvadi (Chairman/CEO, 40-year founder) and Allison (CFO) intact all four quarters; continuity of voice, which makes the consistency of the recovery message more weighty.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

interrogator_dossier:
# Insperity (NSP) — Forensic Dossier

*Transcripts: Q1 2025 (2025-04-29) → Q4 2025 (2026-02-10). Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM as of 2026-03-31. Catalyst status cross-checked against the Q1 2026 print reported post-transcript (April/May 2026).*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story over these four quarters is a single, continuous **deterioration-and-reassurance loop** around one variable management did not control: medical claims cost.

- **Q1 2025** opened on a hopeful inflection — *"We achieved an important inflection point, reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees"* — then immediately pivoted to damage control as benefits costs blew through budget by $28M (`$12M prior-period runoff + $16M current`). Management framed it as possibly *"a shorter-term increase in utilization,"* hedging against calling it a new normal. The forward story was already being relocated to a **future** event: the Workday partnership.
- **Q2 2025** the claim that it might pass faded. Benefits cost trend ran +9.6% YoY; management raised the full-year cost-trend forecast again and leaned harder on the 2026 recovery: *"we remain confident in our outlook for accelerated growth and improved profitability in 2026."* HRScale (the Workday joint product) was formally branded.
- **Q3 2025** was the capitulation quarter. Adjusted EPS went **negative (-$0.20)**, EBITDA collapsed to $10M, and management conceded the cost shock was structural and **industry-wide**: *"the prevailing view in the industry is that the higher claims trend experienced in 2025 will persist in 2026."* New, almost exculpatory drivers appeared — *"the use of AI tools by health care providers has emerged as an additional contributor"* (upcoding). The **new UHC contract** (large-claim pooling cut to $500K from 1/1/2026) was announced as the cost-side fix.
- **Q4 2025** completed the pivot from growth-company to **turnaround/margin-recovery** story. The headline became *"stability and long-term value creation,"* a 4%-of-non-sales-staff RIF, accelerated sales-office consolidation, and a credit facility amendment that **raised the max leverage ratio from 3.0x to 3.75x EBITDA** — a defensive liquidity move. The growth narrative was explicitly deferred: HR360 margin recovery is "the centerpiece of 2026," and HRScale revenue is a **2027** event (*"I'm obviously really excited about '27 because it's the beginning of serious revenue"*).

**Net arc:** a stable, cash-generative HR-outsourcer was repriced by a sudden, persistent medical-cost shock into a margin-recovery turnaround, with the growth engine (HRScale) pushed out to 2027. The drivers quietly dropped: the early-2025 "return to WSEE growth" thesis is gone — FY26 is now guided to **-1.5% to +1.5% WSEE growth**.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | What the financials show | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "improved profitability in 2026" / margin recovery | Q2–Q4 '25 | FY25 NI **-$7M** (EPS -$0.18) vs $91M (2024) and $171M (2023). FY26 guide cut to adj EPS $1.60-$2.60 from $1.69-$2.72; **Q2'26 possible loss** | **Partially true but weaker than sold.** Recovery is real in direction, smaller in magnitude; the Q1'26 print already forced a guide-down. |
| Benefits cost trend will "taper in 2H 2025" | Q2 '25 | Did NOT taper — Q3 trend +9.1%, EPS went negative | **Refuted by their own Q3.** Management's own forecasting credibility took the hit here. |
| Pricing actions will realign margin | Q1–Q4 '25 | GP/WSEE/month fell **$338 (Q1) → $240 (Q2) → $208 (Q3) → $183 (Q4)** — a relentless slide all year despite repricing | **Pricing lagged cost all year.** Only ~40% of the book had been repriced by year-end ("60% yet to receive applicable pricing"). |
| Strong client retention | All | Retention 99%/month, full-year ~83% (Q4) — genuinely held | **True — the franchise's best evidence.** Co-employment switching costs are real. |
| Capital return is sustainable | All | Paid **$90M dividends** in FY25 into a **-$7M NI / 67M TTM FCF** year; adjusted cash fell $124M→$57M across 2025 | **Stretched.** Dividend ($2.40/yr, ~6.6% yield) is being funded out of the balance sheet, not earnings, during the trough. |
| Net cash / solid balance sheet | — | **NET CASH ~$125M** (cash $555M > debt $430M, 2026-03-31); Altman-Z 4.0 | **True and load-bearing for the SoP.** |
| HRScale is "an engine for growth" | Q3–Q4 '25 | $58M invested in 2025, all P&L drag; **zero revenue contribution**; serious revenue not until 2027 | **Aspirational.** A real option, but it has been a cost center, not a driver, for two years. |

The narrative is **richer than the trajectory justifies on growth and margin timing**, and **appropriately matched on retention and balance sheet**. The single biggest divergence: management repeatedly forecast the cost trend would moderate ("taper in 2H'25," "5%-6.5% full year" → ended ~9%), and was wrong each time until Q1'26.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Declining-then-Defensive (Stabilizing in Q4).**

- **Q1 '25 — Cautiously offensive.** *"reestablishing positive growth"* — still selling acceleration; surprise framed as transient.
- **Q2 '25 — Defensive-confident.** *"we remain confident in our outlook for… 2026"* — confidence relocated entirely to a future year; present quarter conceded a miss.
- **Q3 '25 — Defensive/exculpatory.** Heavy use of "industry-wide," "not unique to Insperity," and the novel *"AI tools by health care providers"* attribution — classic externalization of a miss.
- **Q4 '25 — Defensive/operational reset.** Language shifted hard to cost discipline: RIF ("rightsize… approximately 4% of our non-sales staff"), office consolidation, leverage-covenant relief. Tone is steadier but the content is restructuring, not growth.

The shift from **offensive (growth/investment) to defensive (efficiency/cost/macro)** language is unmistakable and monotonic across the four calls. Guidance got *less* specific on the upside (HRScale economics repeatedly "not locked down yet," "$10M a year… don't have a better number") and *more* specific on the downside.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: LOW (improving at the margin).**

- Q1'25 results *"fell below our guidance range."* Q2'25 *"fell slightly under the low end."* Q3'25 *"fell below our forecasted ranges."* Q4'25 came in *"near the middle"* only after backing out a $2.8M charge.
- The cost-trend guide was raised three consecutive quarters (5-6.5% → 6.5-7.5% → +75-100bp → ~9% actual). Management's central forecast variable was wrong, in the same direction, all year.
- The screener beat-rate corroborates: **3/7 quarters (43%)** — a sub-coin-flip hit rate.
- **No sandbagging pattern** — the misses are real misses, not conservative guides. The lone credibility green-shoot: Q4'25 landed mid-range, and the post-transcript **Q1'26 adjusted EBITDA of $103M actually came in roughly flat-to-up** even as EPS missed — suggesting the cost line may finally be stabilizing (benefits cost trend +5% in Q1'26 vs ~9% in 2025). But they **still cut the FY26 EPS guide** on the very next print. Credibility is recovering off a low base, not restored.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"Will pricing stick / will attrition spike?"** (Jeff Martin, Q1'25) — management deflected to "strategically selected accounts" and "we've managed this many times." The Q4'25 admission that **attrition ran higher** ("company-initiated nonrenewals," retention down to ~83%) validated the analyst worry.
- **"What's the range of healthcare-cost outcomes in guidance?"** (Polkowitz, Q2'25) — Allison non-answered: *"I personally don't get too caught up in… the actual specific quarterly trend."* This deflection preceded the Q3 negative-EPS shock.
- **HRScale/Workday economics** (Marcon, Sommer, repeated) — management never quantified margin, pricing, or ramp ("haven't locked that down yet," "not time yet to convert that into sales per salesperson"). Two years in, the flagship growth story remains **unmodeled**.
- **Workday leadership change** (Marcon, Q4'25) — flagged as a risk to the partnership; management waved it off. Worth monitoring.

The pattern: every topic analysts pushed hardest on (cost trend, pricing/attrition trade-off, HRScale economics) is exactly where management was vaguest — and each later turned into the bad news.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **GP/WSEE/month fell every single quarter of 2025** ($338→$183) — the core unit economic deteriorated monotonically.
🔴 **$90M dividends paid into a loss year**; adjusted cash drawn from $124M to $57M — capital return is balance-sheet-funded at the trough.
🔴 **Leverage covenant loosened to 3.75x** and facility upsized to $750M (Q4'25) — a defensive, not opportunistic, financing move.
🔴 **Three consecutive cost-trend guide-ups**, then a fourth FY-EPS guide-down on the post-transcript Q1'26 print (-13% stock reaction) — forecasting credibility on the key variable is poor.
🔴 **HRScale = pure cost for 2 years** ($58M in '25), revenue deferred to 2027 — a perpetually-receding catalyst.
🔴 **WSEE base shrinking** (FY26 guided -1.5% to +1.5%; Q1'26 -1% YoY) — the volume engine has stalled.

🟢 **Net cash ~$125M** and Altman-Z 4.0 — no solvency risk; this is a turnaround with a fortress balance sheet, not a distressed name.
🟢 **Client retention held at ~99%/month** through the worst of it — co-employment switching costs are intact.
🟢 **Genuine insider conviction** — 23 open-market buys (572,566 sh) vs 7 sells (46,256 sh); the grant-inflated 12.38 ratio is noise, but open-market net buying at these prices is a real signal.
🟢 **Cost trend finally moderating** — Q1'26 benefits cost +5% vs ~9% in 2025; the UHC $500K pooling change (effective 1/1/26) is doing some work.
🟢 **Operating-expense discipline** — opex down ~3% FY25, declining sequentially every quarter; management is executing the cost side.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Vocabulary migrated from "growth" to "recovery"/"stability"/"value creation."** Q1'25 led with an "inflection point"; Q4'25 led with *"stability and long-term value creation"* and a 40th-anniversary framing — the language of a maturing, defensive franchise.
- **The "AI" pivot cuts both ways.** Q3'25 introduced AI as a *cost villain* (provider upcoding inflating claims). Simultaneously, management invoked AI as the existential question for its *own* end-market — *"we're on the front end of AI"*, three years of low single-digit net hiring in the SMB base. Sarvadi's tell: *"we haven't seen any of it yet where the malaise… is rooted in specific AI job replacement."* That is the terminal-value question hiding under the cyclical one.
- **Capital allocation tone hardened.** Buybacks stayed token (~$19M/yr, just offsetting dilution) while the dividend was defended — a board choosing yield-signal over opportunistic repurchase even at a 52-week-low-adjacent price. Reads as defending the shareholder base, not maximizing per-share value.
- **HRScale economics stayed deliberately fuzzy across all four calls** — repeated "haven't locked that down," "don't have a better number than $10M a year." Two years of investment without a unit-economic disclosure is itself a signal.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING** (growth-company → margin-recovery turnaround), on a **STABLE-to-improving** operational footing but a **DETERIORATED** earnings base.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** the entire thesis rests on the **2026 margin recovery actually converging** — and the post-transcript Q1'26 print already forced a **guide-down and signaled a possible Q2'26 loss**, with the stock falling ~13%. The cost trend is moderating (+5% vs ~9%) but pricing still lags, only ~40% of the book is repriced, and the WSEE base is shrinking. This is a real franchise with a fortress balance sheet (net cash, 99% retention) trading at a fear-print — but the catalyst is **already partially fired and disappointing**, so the asymmetry is "slow grind to normalized earnings" rather than a dated event-driven re-rate.

**Economic moat:** **NARROW, ERODING.** Moat type = **switching-costs** (co-employment/PEO lock-in, validated by 99% monthly retention). But the *width* is set by returns, and the evidence is deteriorating: ROE -1302.6% (breakeven-distorted), net margin -0.1%, and GP/WSEE collapsing $338→$183 across 2025. The franchise can retain clients but **could not hold price against a cost shock** — pricing power is real but thin and lagged. A moat that retains volume but cannot defend unit margin is **eroding at the economics even as it holds at the logo**.

**Secular threat:** **Manageable-to-material, not terminal.** The structural overhang is **labor-arbitrage-deflation / AI-displacement of SMB headcount** — NSP's revenue scales with *worksite-employee count*, and a third consecutive year of near-zero SMB net hiring (management's own *"I would have never dreamt that"*) plus the AI-on-the-front-end risk caps the volume engine. This is a slow drag on the growth rate, not a melt of the terminal base; the medical-cost shock that crushed 2025 is **cyclical/industry-wide**, not secular, and is already moderating. The cheapness is therefore closer to a **fear-print on a cyclical+self-inflicted margin trough** than a structurally-shrinking base — but the eroding unit margin and stalled volume keep it out of "wide-moat compounder" territory.

interrogator_dossier:
# INTERROGATOR DOSSIER — Insperity, Inc. (NSP)

*Forensic review of five consecutive quarterly calls (Q1 2025 → Q1 2026), cross-referenced against the screener financial trajectory. Sector: Industrials (commercial services / PEO). Signal: deep_value.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story over these five calls is a **controlled demolition of a once-pristine PEO margin model, followed by a scripted recovery narrative** — and the arc is unusually clean because management never hid the wound, they just kept re-sizing it.

- **Q1 2025 (Apr-29):** The call opens on what was *supposed* to be a growth-reacceleration story. Management had "achieved an important inflection point, reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees." That thesis is immediately overrun by two shocks introduced in the same call: (a) tariff/policy-driven SMB sentiment collapse ("66% of respondents expect the economic climate to have a negative effect... up from only 29% in January"), and (b) the first appearance of the benefits-cost monster — Q1 benefits exceeded budget by **$28M** ($12M prior-period claims runoff + $16M current), described as "an unprecedented occurrence." The Workday partnership (HRScale) is the forward growth engine introduced here.
- **Q2 2025 (Aug-01):** Benefits cost is now the *whole story*. Q2 exceeded forecast by another **$12M**, driven by GLP-1/specialty pharmacy. The tone pivots hard from growth to **margin defense** — pricing increases, plan-design changes, and "the negotiation of the anticipated renewal of our contract with UHC." Growth is explicitly deprioritized: "we're not anticipating much support from the client growth."
- **Q3 2025 (Nov-04):** The bottom. Adjusted EPS **−$0.20**, adjusted EBITDA **$10M**, benefits overage **$20M**. Management concedes the trend is structural, not a spike: "the prevailing view in the industry is that the higher claims trend experienced in 2025 will persist in 2026." A new exogenous culprit appears — **AI-driven upcoding by providers** ("the use of AI tools by health care providers has emerged as an additional contributor"). HRScale is formally launched.
- **Q4 2025 (Feb-10):** The capitulation quarter dressed as a turnaround. Adjusted EPS **−$0.60**, adjusted EBITDA **−$13M**. But the narrative flips to recovery: "measurable margin recovery" achieved in the fall campaign, a **new UHC contract** (pooling limit cut $1M→$500K), 4% non-sales workforce cut, credit facility amended (capacity $650M→$750M, max leverage 3x→3.75x). Growth guidance for 2026 set at **−1.5% to +1.5%** — i.e. shrink.
- **Q1 2026 (Apr-30):** The proof-of-recovery quarter, and it largely delivers. Adjusted EPS **$1.31** (above midpoint), adjusted EBITDA **$103M** (+1% YoY), gross profit decline narrowed to **−3%** vs the −21% of Q4. Benefits cost trend decelerated from ~9% to **5%**. The story is now explicitly a **3-year plan: year-1 margin recovery → year-2 balanced growth → year-3 (2027) HRScale revenue inflection.** AI is reframed from threat (provider upcoding) to opportunity (Insperity's own AI initiatives).

**Drivers introduced/dropped:** Workday/HRScale was introduced as a growth catalyst in Q1-25 and *survived* the whole arc, but its payoff was serially pushed right — from "margin builder in the future" (Q1-25) to beta-onboarding in March-2026 with "serious revenue" not until **2027** (Q4-25/Q1-26). The original 2025 growth-reacceleration thesis was quietly **abandoned**; "growth" was redefined from headcount expansion to margin recovery.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees" | Q1-25 | FY25 rev +3.5%; WSE growth ended FY25 at ~+1%; 2026 guided −1.5% to +1.5% | **Overstated** — growth never materialized; the base is now *shrinking* |
| "Higher-than-expected benefits costs... an unprecedented occurrence" | Q1-25 | FY24 NI $91M → FY25 NI **−$7M**; EPS $2.39 → **−$0.18**; net margin −0.1% | **Confirmed and then some** — this single line item vaporized all GAAP earnings |
| Margin recovery "on track"; "step-up in gross profit key drivers" | Q4-25 | Q1-26 GP decline narrowed to −3% (vs −21% in Q4-25); benefits trend 9%→5%; adj EBITDA $103M (+1% YoY) | **Corroborated** — the clearest case where the narrative matched the numbers |
| Capital return / shareholder-friendly | All | Paid $90M dividends FY25, bought back $19M stock; **NET CASH $125M** (cash $555M > debt $430M) at 2026-03-31 | **Confirmed** — they kept the dividend through a loss year; balance sheet remains a fortress |
| ROIC / returns quality | — | Screener ROIC **16.5%** even at the trough; ROE −1303% is a denominator artifact (near-zero equity), NOT economic | Returns base intact; the ROE figure is **noise**, not signal |
| HRScale "engine for growth," "significant catalyst" | Q3-25/Q1-26 | Zero revenue contribution to date; $58M invested in 2025 alone, all through the P&L; "serious revenue" = 2027 | **Story richer than numbers** — real option, but unmonetized and dated to 2027 |
| Forward EPS growth | — | Consensus fwd EPS growth **+36.1%**; FY26 guide adj EPS $1.60–$2.60, adj EBITDA $170–230M | Recovery is real but the *range is enormous* (±$1.00 EPS) — low conviction |

**Where the story is richer than the trajectory justifies:** HRScale. It has absorbed real cash for two years with no revenue, and the payoff date keeps sliding to 2027. **Where it is poorer:** the actual margin-recovery execution in Q1-26 is *better* than the beaten-down narrative implied — management under-promised on the trough and the recovery is ahead of the bears' fears.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Declining through Q3-25, then Rising into Q1-26 (V-shaped).**

- **Q1-25 — defensive/surprised:** "We are obviously disappointed by the emergence of higher benefits costs." Offensive growth language already yielding to cost language.
- **Q2-25 — defensive, hedging:** "I personally don't get too caught up in what the actual specific quarterly trend is." Repeated externalization ("not unique to Insperity," "commonplace to address current market conditions").
- **Q3-25 — defensive, bottom:** the AI-upcoding explanation is the tell — management reaching for a *novel external cause* to explain a fourth consecutive miss. Confidence visibly low; guidance range absurdly wide (FY adj EPS $0.84–$1.47).
- **Q4-25 — pivoting, scripted-confident:** "we accomplished this key objective," "decisive actions." A deliberate re-framing toward a 3-year plan and a 40th-anniversary narrative — classic management move to change the subject from the trailing P&L.
- **Q1-26 — genuinely more confident, and earned:** "We are pleased with our Q1 earnings results." For the first time in the arc the confidence is *backed by a beat* (adj EPS above midpoint, EBITDA +1% YoY). Guidance specificity returns.

Shift from **offensive → defensive (Q1-Q3 2025) → cautiously offensive (Q1-26)**. The defensiveness was warranted; the renewed confidence is, for once, supported by a printed number.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: LOW through 2025, improving to MEDIUM by Q1-26.**

Management **missed its own guidance four quarters running** in 2025 — Q1 "fell below our guidance range," Q2 "fell slightly under the low end," Q3 "fell below our forecasted ranges," and the full-year benefits trend blew through every successive range they set ("5%–6.5%" → "6.5%–7.5%" → "75–100bps higher" → ~9% actual). The screener's **beat rate of 3/7 quarters (43%)** confirms a sub-coin-flip delivery record. There is **no sandbagging pattern** in 2025 — they genuinely could not forecast their own largest cost line, which is the single most damning credibility fact in this file.

The redemption: **Q1-26 came in *above* the midpoint** of guidance, and benefits cost (5% vs the ~9% run-rate) beat. One quarter does not reset a four-quarter miss streak, but the *direction* of credibility is now positive. Crucially, management flagged that the UHC contract benefit is **back-end loaded** ("more back-end loaded than... we have maybe clearly communicated... some earnings estimates that are out there on the analyst side") — a setup that either becomes a H2 beat or a fresh miss. **Score: Medium, on probation.**

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"Is this benefits trend permanent or transitory?"** — pushed every quarter (Polkowitz/JPM, Marcon). Management's answer migrated from "unprecedented spike" (Q1) to "will persist in 2026" (Q3) — i.e. the analysts who pressed on permanence were **right**, management was slow.
- **HRScale/Workday profitability and timing** — Jeff Martin (ROTH) and Polkowitz repeatedly pressed "how should we think about profitability" and "is the $150M aggregate investment still right." Management consistently **non-answered** ("we haven't locked that down yet," "we don't have a better number right now"). Two years in, the unit economics are still undisclosed — a persistent deflection.
- **Pricing → attrition risk** — analysts worried higher pricing would spike churn. Management insisted retention would hold; FY25 retention came in ~83% (vs ~81% prior), so this one **held up**.
- **Workday leadership change** (Q4-25, Marcon) — flagged as a relationship risk; management waved it off. Unresolved third-party dependency.

The topic analysts pushed on that became bad news: **benefits permanence.** The topic they keep pushing that management still won't quantify: **HRScale economics.**

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Four consecutive guidance misses in 2025** with a cost line management repeatedly mis-forecast — the core PEO value-prop is *managing benefits risk for SMBs*, and they failed to price their own risk for a full year.
🔴 **FY25 GAAP net loss (−$7M, EPS −$0.18)** and TTM diluted EPS −$0.67 — earnings power genuinely impaired, not merely optically cheap.
🔴 **Shrinking base:** WSE guided −1.5% to +1.5% for 2026; new-client WSE −7% YoY in Q1-26; three straight years of "very low single-digit net gain" in the client base. The growth engine is stalled independent of benefits.
🔴 **Tax-rate volatility** (41% in Q1-26 vs 29% prior) from depressed stock price reducing comp-vesting deductions — a self-reinforcing low-stock-price drag on reported EPS.
🔴 **HRScale = two years of P&L cash burn ($58M in 2025) for a 2027 payoff** — a real option, but the date keeps sliding right.

🟢 **Net cash $125M** (cash $555M > total debt $430M) — a genuine SoP add-back and downside cushion; the dividend was *never* cut through the loss year.
🟢 **Q1-26 margin-recovery beat** — benefits trend 9%→5%, GP decline −21%→−3%, adj EBITDA +1% YoY. The recovery thesis is printing.
🟢 **Real insider conviction** — open-market only: **23 buys (572,566 sh) vs 7 sells (46,256 sh)**. Insiders bought ~12x the dollar value they sold *into the trough*. This is the strongest single bull tell in the file.
🟢 **New UHC contract structurally de-risks the tail** — pooling limit $1M→$500K caps catastrophic-claim exposure; back-end-loaded benefit sets up H2-26.
🟢 **Cost discipline** — opex down ~3% FY25, further cuts targeted; $9M restructuring already taken in Q1-26.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The AI framing flips 180°.** In Q3-25, AI is a *cost villain* — providers using AI to upcode claims against Insperity. By Q1-26, AI is an *Insperity opportunity* ("our AI initiatives," "we're on the front end of AI"). Watch this: it's a tell that management reframes external forces to fit the quarter's narrative. The genuinely interesting subtext (Q3-25, Allison) is that AI-driven white-collar layoffs could *shrink Insperity's own addressable headcount* — a structural risk to a co-employment model that bills per worksite employee.
- **"Success penalty" language** (Q1-26): management admits clients they "helped grow" outgrow the small-business PEO and leave — HRScale is explicitly the *retention fix* for graduating clients, not just new growth. That reframes HRScale from offense to **defense against structural churn up-market.**
- **Capitalization shift:** beginning Q3-25, Workday costs crossed the software-cap threshold ($5M capitalized in Q1-26). Watch for EPS being flattered by capitalizing what was previously expensed — a quiet quality-of-earnings degradation as the recovery is sold.
- **Client mix is doing the heavy lifting on margin** (Q1-26, Allison): "the biggest impact is the client mix" — i.e. low-profit clients terminating, high-profit staying. Margin recovery is partly **survivorship of the shrinking base**, not pure pricing power.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (margin-recovery turnaround off a confirmed earnings trough), with a STABLE balance sheet underneath.**

The single most important thing the director must weigh: **this is a self-help margin-recovery story whose Q1-26 print is genuinely better than the bear case — but it sits on a structurally non-growing (currently shrinking) worksite-employee base, and the load-bearing recovery catalyst (the UHC contract) is already LIVE and partly in the price.** The asymmetry is no longer "will it survive" (the net-cash balance sheet and the Q1-26 beat answer that) but "will margin recover to *historical* levels (gross profit/WSE back toward $400+/mo) on a *flat-to-shrinking* unit base." If yes, FY26 adj EBITDA lands in the $200M+ upper half and EPS normalizes toward the high-$2s/low-$3s — meaningful upside from $36. If the H2 back-end-loaded UHC benefit disappoints, the four-miss credibility record reasserts and the stock re-tests the trough.

**Economic moat — NARROW, switching-costs type, STABLE (not eroding).** The PEO model has genuine switching costs (co-employment, benefits administration, compliance entanglement) and **ROIC held at 16.5% even at the GAAP-loss trough** — the durable earnings base did not break, it was margin-compressed by an exogenous medical-cost shock. The gross-margin contraction (13.2%, sub-2.5σ below historical peak) is **cyclical/exogenous (a medical-claims spike), not a competitive-moat erosion** — and Q1-26 shows it already reflecting. This is NOT a high-but-falling-ROIC eroding moat; returns were stable, margins took a one-time hit that is recovering. Moat is narrow because ADP/Paychex out-scale on technology and the SMB base is churny ("success penalty").

**Secular threat — MANAGEABLE, not terminal.** The relevant structural force is **AI-driven white-collar headcount deflation** (labor-arbitrage / ai-displacement adjacent): a co-employment model that bills per worksite employee is exposed if AI structurally shrinks SMB payrolls. But (a) management credibly notes no evidence of AI job-replacement in its base yet, (b) AI historically *spawns* new SMB formation (a partial offset), and (c) HRScale moves Insperity *up-market* to larger, stickier accounts. The medical-cost shock that caused the cheapness is **cyclical/idiosyncratic, not secular** — this is a fear-print on a recovering franchise, **not a structurally-shrinking base value trap.** The genuine caution is the *flat unit base*, which caps the re-rate ceiling.
